stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Yes — Thomas Flynn's 1881 will directly names 'my eldest son Patrick Flynn' as principal heir, answering the parentage question with primary direct evidence from the father himself. Census co-enumerations in 1850, 1860, and 1870 independently corroborate continued co-residence in the same household across three decades.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: FamilySearch searched for censuses (1850–1900), probate, and death records; Ancestry and MyHeritage cross-checked for the 1850 census. No additional repository holds accessible Schuylkill County death records for this period (documented destroyed 1920, no PA State Archives duplicates). Catholic baptismal records are documented as fragmentary for this county and period. Naturalization records for Thomas Flynn were assessed as low value (they rarely name children) and were a rational skip given three converging sources already in hand.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: Original census images accessed via FamilySearch for 1850, 1860, 1870, 1880, and 1900 enumerations. Ancestry's 1850 index confirmed as consistent with the FamilySearch original. Thomas Flynn's will accessed as original image (Schuylkill County Register of Wills, Will Book G, p. 214).
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Three independent sources with distinct record types and informants: (1) 1850 census — unknown household informant reporting to enumerator, indirect evidence; (2) 1860 census — different enumerator and informant, indirect evidence; (3) Thomas Flynn's 1881 will — Thomas Flynn himself as informant, direct evidence. Different record creation processes and institutional origins; genuine independence satisfied.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: Yes — Thomas Flynn's will is an original source with primary information: the testator directly names his own children in a legal document. This is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship at the highest attainable information quality for this record type. Census co-enumerations are original sources providing indirect evidence.
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: No conflicts identified across any source. All evidence consistently implies or directly states Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father. No competing parentage candidates appear in any record examined.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Low. Thomas Flynn's will provides direct primary evidence — the father himself names Patrick as his eldest son in a legal instrument. The two decisive records that could have added primary evidence (death certificate, baptismal record) are documented as inaccessible, not merely unsearched. No accessible, unexamined record type is plausible to name a different father.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

results.name: test_no_exhaustive_declaration
results.error: skipped: not a no-exhaustive-declaration scenario

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All factual claims are grounded in the project state and tool responses. The skill correctly identified that skipped items (death certificate, baptismal records) are permitted when documented as inaccessible rather than merely unsearched. The will (src_005) is correctly cited as providing direct primary evidence from Thomas Flynn naming Patrick as eldest son. Census sources (1850, 1860, 1870) are correctly characterized as independent indirect evidence. The overturn risk assessment is sound: with direct evidence from the father and documented inaccessibility of the only other decisive records, the declaration is defensible.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill fully addressed the user's request to evaluate whether the parentage question can be declared exhaustive given one skipped item and three completed items. All five threshold questions were explicitly evaluated and answered. All seven stop criteria were assessed with specific reference to project log entries and evidence. The declaration was written and persisted via the research_append tool.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The single MCP call (mcp__genealogy__research_append) passed the correct arguments: projectPath, section='questions', op='update', entryId='q_001', and fields with the exhaustive_declaration object containing declared=true, justification, log_entry_ids, and all seven stop_criteria keys. The declaration structure matches the expected schema and references specific log entries. No fixture_not_found errors occurred.

dimensions.name: Threshold reasoning
dimensions.rationale: The skill explicitly evaluated all five threshold questions (research question answered, broad range of record types, relevant strategies employed, derivatives replaced with originals, conflicts resolved) with specific grounding in the project state. For threshold 4, the skill correctly noted original FamilySearch images for censuses and the will. The reasoning that 'all five pass' is grounded in concrete evidence rather than generic claims. The skill did not declare until confirming all thresholds were met.

dimensions.name: Declaration honesty
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly distinguished between skipped items with documented reasons (death certificate destroyed in 1920 fire, baptismal records fragmentary) and unsearched items. The declaration resists premature optimization: it acknowledges the skipped death certificate but honestly justifies why it does not block the declaration (records are documented as inaccessible, not merely difficult to find). The overturn_risk criterion explicitly states 'Low' with clear reasoning: the will provides direct evidence and no accessible alternative exists that could name a different father.

dimensions.name: Stop criteria coverage
dimensions.rationale: All seven stop_criteria keys are present with substantive 1–2 sentence assessments tied to specific project evidence: goal_alignment references the 1881 will and census co-residence; repository_breadth names FamilySearch, Ancestry, MyHeritage, and documents the 1920 fire; original_substitution cites Will Book G p. 214 and FamilySearch image dates; independent_verification distinguishes three separate informants and record types; evidence_class identifies the will as direct primary evidence; conflict_resolution confirms no competing candidates; overturn_risk acknowledges documented inaccessibility as distinct from mere difficulty. No keys are missing or generic.
